coredata.dc:title: Embedded iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots within g-C3N4 as an exceptional PMS photocatalytic activator for sulfamethoxazole degradation: The key role of FeN bridge
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Pingping
Cao, Xiao
Gu, Lin
Yu, Haixiang
Wu, Feiyan
Liu, Yifan
Liu, Xinyue
Gao, Yutao
Zhang, Hanlin
coredata.dc:description:
The covalent modification of graphite-phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a promising way to improve its photocatalytic performance. Although CQDs can act as charge mediator for electrons storage and transfer, it is crucial to construct efficient interfaces, which serves as active sites for tight connect with g-C3N4 and fast release of electrons. Herein, a novel iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe, N-CQDs) assembled g-C3N4 composite (FNCCN) was constructed via FeN bridge by hydrothermal and thermal polymerization methods. The FNCNN rivaled popular nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) combined with g-C3N4 composites for peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation and degradation of sulfamethoxazole (SMX). The SMX degradation rate (0.0963 min−1) is 5.38 and 3.15 times higher than that of g-C3N4 and N-CQDs/g-C3N4. The characterization and DFT calculation results suggest that Fe, N-CQDs was successfully incorporated into carbon matrix of g-C3N4 via FeN covalent bond, which joints the two counterparts closely and creates electron transport channels for higher electrons transfer efficiency. The FeN coordinated structure promotes PMS reduction on Fe2+ of FeN sites for
HO and SO4
- production and expedited photo carries separation through Fe3+/Fe2+ cycling. In addition, the degradation efficiency, ROS identification, EPR test and degradation pathway of SMX was determined, and the intermediate toxicity of degradation products was predicted. This work constructed a tight atomic-level connection between hydrophobic g-C3N4 and hydrophilic CQDs, which improved an effective strategy for accelerating electron transport and efficient photocatalytic activation of PMS.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The covalent modification of graphite-phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a promising way to improve its photocatalytic performance. Although CQDs can act as charge mediator for electrons storage and transfer, it is crucial to construct efficient interfaces, which serves as active sites for tight connect with g-C3N4 and fast release of electrons. Herein, a novel iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe, N-CQDs) assembled g-C3N4 composite (FNCCN) was constructed via FeN bridge by hydrothermal and thermal polymerization methods. The FNCNN rivaled popular nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) combined with g-C3N4 composites for peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation and degradation of sulfamethoxazole (SMX). The SMX degradation rate (0.0963 min−1) is 5.38 and 3.15 times higher than that of g-C3N4 and N-CQDs/g-C3N4. The characterization and DFT calculation results suggest that Fe, N-CQDs was successfully incorporated into carbon matrix of g-C3N4 via FeN covalent bond, which joints the two counterparts closely and creates electron transport channels for higher electrons transfer efficiency. The FeN coordinated structure promotes PMS reduction on Fe2+ of FeN sites for
HO and SO4
- production and expedited photo carries separation through Fe3+/Fe2+ cycling. In addition, the degradation efficiency, ROS identification, EPR test and degradation pathway of SMX was determined, and the intermediate toxicity of degradation products was predicted. This work constructed a tight atomic-level connection between hydrophobic g-C3N4 and hydrophilic CQDs, which improved an effective strategy for accelerating electron transport and efficient photocatalytic activation of PMS.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfamethoxazole (SMX), a representative sulfonamide antibiotic characterized by the chemical formula C10H11N3O3S, is extensively employed in the treatment of infections related to the urinary tract, respiratory system, and various other ailments [1]. During practical usage, only 10–50 % of it is absorbed by the body, with the remaining unmetabolized portion typically being discharged into the sewage system [2]. However, due to the presence of the stable sulfonamide group and oxazole ring in its molecular structure (Fig. S1), SMX is characterized by strong persistence and low biodegradability, making it difficult to effectively remove through conventional wastewater treatment methods [3,4]. The residuals of SMX not only lead to bacterial resistance and reduce the level of drug treatment, but also pose certain risks to the ecosystem and human health [5,6]. To this end, it is essential to develop more efficient treatment methods to eliminate SMX.

Of late years, the sulfate radical (SO4
−) based Advanced Oxidation Processes (SR-AOPs) has gained much attention in contaminated water treatment. In comparison to hydroxyl radicals (
OH), SO4
− has a higher redox potential (2.5–3.1 V), wider pH range (3–9) and longer half-life (30–40 μs) [7,8]. To date, persulfate have been activated by various methods, including transition metal ions, ultrasonic irradiation, thermal treatment and photocatalysis [9]. Among these methods, visible-light-driven photocatalytic activation has attracted much more interests owing to its use of green solar energy and mild reaction conditions [10].

As a polymeric n-type semiconductor material, graphite-phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with stable chemical properties and suitable band gap has been gradually used for persulfate activation. However, the deficiencies of bulk g-C3N4 still exist, such as poor electron transport, narrow visible light utilization range, and quick recombination rate of photogenerated carriers, which seriously affect their application in persulfate activation [11]. Therefore, it is often necessary to improve photocatalytic ability through precious metal deposition, heteroatomic doping and semiconductor coupling [12–14].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new emerging nano 0D materials, possess outstanding up-conversion photoluminescence characteristics [15]. Generally, the impact of interfacial interactions on catalytic performance cannot be overlooked. Despite the significant improvement in the light absorption range of g-C3N4 achieved by non-covalent modifications (π-π stacking) with CQDs, the inherent hydrophobic issues of g-C3N4 still lead to poor interfacial coupling and restricted charge transfer between the two components [16]. These limitations severely affect the performance of the composite material. Fortunately, recent studies have shown that the covalent bonds, including NC, CCl, and NP, formed by heteroatom-doped CQDs covalently modifying g-C3N4 can serve as an electron transport channels to achieve more effective and faster charge transfer [17–19].

Notwithstanding, the electron transfer channels forged by these covalent bonds are predominantly nonmetallic, merely functioning as connectors between CQDs and g-C3N4. The mechanism of charge transfer facilitated by these bonds remains elusive, with CQDs primarily functioning as electron acceptors in the majority of instances. Reports indicate that CQDs, when serving as electron mediators, typically exhibit rapid electron storage rates but comparatively slower release rates [20]. Therefore, it is imperative to establish a bridge that not only facilitates the hybridization of CQDs with g-C3N4 but also enables electron donation for efficient PMS activation, leading to effective separation of electron-hole pairs. Associating with the strong electron extracting essence induced by N coordinated Fe single atom (FeN) catalyst in PMS activation, the electron transfer is anticipated to be expedited when Fe atoms are employed for bridge construction. The Fe dopants present in CQDs, featuring variable valence states and unoccupied orbitals, have the capability to form chelates with the conjugated N atoms within g-C3N4. Notably, the FeN coordination bond forged between g-C3N4 and iron species has been demonstrated to expedite the migration of reductive photogenerated electrons towards Fe3+ sites, thereby fostering the Fe3+/Fe2+ cycling and guaranteeing sustained activation of PMS [21,22].

Consequently, we ponder the possibility of introducing metallic Fe into CQDs to establish a valent bond with g-C3N4, and whether a comparable FeN coordination pattern can be formed between CQDs and g-C3N4. This strategy not only strengthen the interfacial connectivity but also facilitates the quick transfer of photogenerated electrons via the FeN bonds, thereby accelerating the separation of photogenerated carriers and bolstering sustained PMS activation through the cycling of Fe3+ and Fe2+. To the best of our understanding, reports on successfully integrating Fe-doped CQDs within g-C3N4 while simultaneously expediting electron transfers via FeN coordination bridges are scarcely available.

In this study, we aimed to verify the aforementioned hypothesis by fabricating iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe, N-CQDs) embedded g-C3N4, engineered to expedite PMS activation for the efficacious degradation of SMX. The doping of N within the Fe, N-CQDs facilitated conditions that were favorable for the chelation of Fe. The formation of FeN bridges between Fe, N-CQDs and g-C3N4 was corroborated through rigorous material characterization and DFT formation energy calculations. By combing catalytic degradation, ROS identification, and EPR testing, we elucidated the synergistic catalytic mechanism underlying photocatalysis and PMS activation. The degradation pathway of pollutants was ascertained through a combination of DFT theoretical calculations and LC-MS. Finally, the potential toxicity of degradation intermediates was anticipated using TEST toxicity assessment software.
All chemicals were provided in Supporting Information Text S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fe, N-CQDs was prepared by a hydrothermal method based on previously report [23]. In detail, CA (3.6 g), DETA (0.6 mL) and FeCl3·6H2O (0.9 g) were dissolved in deionized water (60 mL) and heated at 210 ℃ for 12 h in autoclave. Upon reaching room temperature, the obtained solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane to separate and remove larger particle impurities. Subsequently, the filtered solution was dialyzed in deionized water with a dialysis bag (MWCO 500 Da) for 24 h and change the water every 6 h to obtain an aqueous solution of Fe, N-CQDs. Finally, a solid powder Fe, N-CQDs was obtained by freeze-drying. N-CQDs was prepared following the same process without the addition of FeCl3·6H2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of FNCCN, NCCN and CN
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process for the composites was shown in Fig. 1 . To begin, a selected amount of urea (3 g) and Fe, N-CQDs (3 mg, 5 mg, 9 mg) were grounded evenly in a mortar and transferred to the crucible. Then, the crucible was calcinated from room temperature to 550 ℃ and maintained at this temperature for 3 h in a muffle oven. The obtained samples were labelled as FNCCN-X (where X denoted the mass of Fe, N-CQDs). Throughout this study, the term FNCCN referred specifically to FNCCN-5, except when stated otherwise. The N-CQDs/g-C3N4 (NCCN) was obtained from N-CQDs and urea following the above method. The pristine g-C3N4 (CN) was synthesized with urea in the absence of Fe, N-CQDs.
A detailed description of the characteristics was provided in Supporting Information Texts S2.

SMX was selected as the target pollutant to evaluate the activation performance of the catalyst. The entire reaction was occurred in a photochemical reactor (JH-GHX -III, Jihui Analytical Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) equipped with a xenon lamp and UV cutoff filters. The temperature was maintained at ambient conditions using a circulating water system. KOH (0.1 M) and H2SO4 (0.1 M) were used to adjust the initial pH of SMX. Typically, 8 mg of the catalyst were added to a 30 mL test tube containing 20 mL of a 5 ppm SMX solution. The mixture was agitated in the dark for 30 min to attain adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the light source was turned on for the photocatalytic reaction and the PMS (0.5 g/L) was added into the test tube. Periodically, 1 mL of the suspension was taken out, immediately mixed with 0.1 mL Na2S2O3 (0.5 M) to quench the photocatalytic reaction and filtered by 0.22 μm water system membrane filters to remove the powder. During the degradation process, varying gradients of reaction parameters were applied to assess their impact on the degradation efficiency of SMX. The samples were analyzed by HPLC at 264 nm. The degradation kinetics of SMX were consistent with a pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Eq. (1)).

where C0 and Ct (mg/L) are the SMX concentrations at the beginning of the photocatalytic experiment and t (min), k1 (min−1) is the rate constant.

In the ROS scavenging experiment, MeOH utilized to quench
OH and SO4
−. TBA, p-BQ, EDTA-2Na and L-histidine served as scavengers for
OH, superoxide radicals(
O2 –), hole (h+) and single oxygen (1O2), respectively.
Comprehensive information regarding the Computational methods employed can be found in Supporting Information Text S3.

The microstructure and morphology of photocatalysts were observed by SEM and TEM. As exhibited in Fig. 2 a-c, the TEM images of CN, NCCN and FNCCN all showed a typical two-dimensional layered structure with a large number of nanopores (marked by yellow circles) on the surface, which were caused by the release of a large amount of ammonia and CO2 during the copolymerization of the precursor [24]. The generation of gases can also hinder the decomposition of Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs during the thermal polymerization process, ensuring the successful doping of Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs [25]. The N-CQDs and Fe, N-CQDs observed on the NCCN and FNCCN surfaces were marked with red circles. The SEM images of CN, NCCN and FNCCN were shown in Fig. 2d-f. The morphology of CN showed two-dimensional lamellae with smooth surface. Upon incorporating Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs into CN, the nanosheet structure remained intact. However, a large number of pores emerged on the surface of the material (as depicted in Fig. 2e-f). Furthermore, the pores observed in FNCCN were slightly more abundant than those in NCCN, suggesting that the introduction of Fe, N-CQDs into CN induced a more pronounced structural distortion within the polymer in comparison to N-CQDs [26]. To provide a clearer understanding of the underlying reasons, we have presented particle size histograms of N-CQDs and Fe, N-CQDs in Fig. S2c and S2f. These histograms reveal that the incorporation of Fe into N-CQDs led to an increase in their radius, ultimately resulting in a larger average particle size. This enlargement in size could potentially explain the more pronounced structural distortion observed when Fe, N-CQDs were introduced into the carbon nitride matrix, as compared to N-CQDs. The elemental mapping of FNCCN were shown in Fig. 2 g, and all of elements were evenly distributed, demonstrating that Fe, N-CQDs were successfully implanted into g-C3N4 and uniformly decorated on the framework.

The XRD patterns of the CN, NCCN, FNCCN were displayed in Fig. 3 a. All of the catalysts had two pronounced diffraction peaks at 13.1◦ and 27.5◦ associated with the (100) plane for in-plane structural packing motifs of tris-triazine units and the (002) plane for interlayer-stacking of conjugated aromatic system, respectively [27]. None of the characteristic peaks of Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs were observed on the XRD pattern of FNCCN and NCCN, which was due to the relatively low mass loading of Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs. After decorating with Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs, the overall intensity of the diffraction peak decreased, indicating that the incorporation of Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs disrupted the periodic arrangement of the fundamental units in carbon nitride. FNCCN had the lowest diffraction peak intensity, suggesting that Fe, N-CQDs were more destructive and caused more defects than N-CQDs. Based on the previous literature, it was speculated that Fe in FNCCN may exist in the form of FeN chemical sites [28].

The FTIR spectra of the CN, NCCN, FNCCN were illustrated in Fig. 3b. All catalysts exhibited a sharp band at 808 cm−1, the strong bond of 1200–1670 cm−1 and the broad peak spanning from 3000 to 3700 cm−1. These corresponded to the bending vibration of tri-s-heptazine or tri-s-triazine units, the stretching vibrational mode of C-N/C = N, and the stretching vibration of –OH, –NH2 & –NH, respectively [29]. These similar curves implied that the basic skeleton of C3N4 did not change after the addition of Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs, correlating well with the XRD results. FNCCN exhibited a reduction in vibrational intensity in the 1200–1670 cm−1 range compared to NCCN. This alteration could be attributed to the formation of FeN coordination bonds between Fe, N-CQDs and g-C3N4 during the thermal polymerization process, resulting in the weakening of the triazine ring skeletal or stretching vibrations in FNCCN [21].

The N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms, along with the BJH pore size distributions, were presented in Fig. 3c and Table. S1. All samples exhibited typical type IV isotherms or H3 hysteresis loops, which indicated the presence of mesoporous structures in CN, NCCN, and FNCCN. The introduction of Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs optimized the surface characteristics of CN. Compared with CN, the specific surface area of NCCN and FNCCN increased from 56.15 m2/g to 80.40 m2/g and 84.59 m2/g, respectively. These was caused by the large amount of gas released by Fe, N-CQDs or N-CQDs and urea during thermal polymerization. Compared to N-CQDs, Fe, N-CQDs can cause the more severe structural aberration of g-C3N4, which may result in the slightly higher surface area of FNCCN than that of NCCN. In addition, the release of gas created abundant pores on the CN surface, which can provide a larger contact interface during the reaction, exposing more active sites and promoting the adsorption of pollutants on the catalyst surface [30,31]. This was verified in the dark reaction adsorption process in Fig. 6a below.

The XPS spectra of CN, NCCN and FNCCN were presented in Fig. 4 . The survey spectra (Fig. S3) revealed the presence of peaks corresponding to N, C and O. Among them, the appearance of the O element peak can be ascribed to the adsorption of O2 and H2O on pristine g-C3N4 [32]. For FNCCN, the appearance of the Fe peak indicated the successful incorporation of Fe, N-CQDs. The C1s spectrum of CN (Fig. 4a) can be separated into two peaks, approximately at 284.8 eV and 288.17 eV, which were assigned to CC/CC and NCH [33]. After doping with N-CQDs and Fe, N-CQDs, a new peak corresponding to C-OH emerged in NCCN (285.91 eV) as well as FNCCN (286.33 eV). This was a result of N-CQDs and Fe, N-CQDs forming transverse heterostructures with CN, resulting in hydroxylation of carbon atoms on the material's surface, confirming the successful incorporation of N-CQDs and Fe, N-CQDs [34]. Compared with CN, the peak position of N 1s orbital of NCCN moved to higher binding energy, attributed to the change of the chemical environment of N atom after the introduction of N-CQDs. The binding energy of N 1s orbital and the relative content of peak of FNCCN also changed greatly compared with NCCN, especially for Triazine N (N-C3), whose relative content decreased from 27.05 % to 23.29 %, and the binding energy position also moved from 399.94 eV to 399.28 eV. These changes were the result of the influence of FeN coordination. Generally, the binding energy of the FeN coordination bond was between 398.3 eV and 399.3 eV [21]. However, this binding energy range overlaps with the binding energy range of Triazine N and Pyridic N, and it was difficult to completely distinguish them. Thus, the N 1s of FNCCN (Fig. 4b) could be segregated into four peaks, positioned at approximately 398.58 eV, 399.28 eV, 400.94 eV and 404.27 eV, representing the Pyridic N (CNC), Triazine N (N-C3)/N-Fe, Tertiary N (CNH), Oxidized N, respectively [35]. In Fig. 4c, the Fe 2p spectra of FNCCN revealed distinct peaks. The peaks centered at 710.16 eV and 722.96 eV represented to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 of Fe2+, whilst the peaks with binding energies of 713.95 and 726.75 eV corresponded to Fe3+. Fe, N-CQDs injected into g-C3N4 through FeN chemical bonding, which can accelerate the migration of reductive photogenerated electrons from g-C3N4 to Fe3+ sites, promoting the Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle and ensuring the sustained activation of PMS.

To confirm the formation of FeN coordination structures in FNCCN, DFT calculations were performed (Fig. 5 ). Before proceeding with the calculations, we undertook an analysis and comparison of the FTIR spectra of Fe, N-CQDs, N-CQDs, and CQDs. This step was crucial to gaining insights into the configurational information of FNCCN, which subsequently informed our approach to the computations. As shown in S2b, the absorption peaks located at 3432, 2935 and 1600 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of –OH/-NHx, CH and CC, respectively [36,37]. Compared with CQDs, Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs showed a new peak corresponding to the amide CN bond emerged at 1350 cm−1. This observation suggested that the addition of DETA during the hydrothermal process effectively passivated CA, leading to the conversion of a portion of CA’s carbonyl groups into amide groups. This conversion improved chelation with iron ions during the hybridization process, as outlined in reference [23]. Zooming in the selected area in Fig. S4a (Fig. S4b), a distinct new peak at 701 cm−1 for Fe, N-CQDs was attributed to FeN bond, indicating that Fe was successfully doped into Fe, N-CQDs by the coordination with N atoms [23,38].

The configuration of the CN was illustrated in Fig. 5a. Combining the FTIR information of Fe, N-CQDs, and based on the structure of graphene, we constructed the configuration of Fe, N-CQDs. The configuration of Fe, N-CQDs was embedded into the framework of CN to construct the possible structures of FNCCN (Fig. 5b–e). We calculated the formation energy of these structures. Generally, the more negative the formation energy, the easier the configuration was to form [39]. The calculation results showed that the structure with FeN coordination had a lower formation energy than other configurations, and the Fe in Fe, N-CQDs was more inclined to coordinate with the N in N-C3 in CN. The formation energy of this structure was −4.003 eV.

The excellent up-conversion photoluminescence performance and light absorption ability of carbon dots can effectively improve the light harvesting ability of photocatalyst [10]. The UV–vis-DRS spectra of CN, NCCN and FNCCN were shown in Fig. 6 a. Compared to CN, after introducing N-CQDs and Fe, N-CQDs into the carbon nitride matrix, the absorption edges of NCCN and FNCCN shifted from 450 nm to 476 nm and 485 nm, respectively. It was clear that the introduction of carbon dots can effectively broaden the spectral response range of the photocatalyst. The enhanced light absorption capability of FNCCN compared to NCCN can be attributed to two reasons. Firstly, the formation of FeN bridges in FNCCN led to a more significant alteration in the electron density distribution and energy gap [40]. This change resulted in improved light absorption properties. Secondly, when compared to N-CQDs, Fe, N-CQDs demonstrated superior light absorption performance in the visible region due to the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) effect, as evident in Fig. S5. The LMCT effect arises from the transfer of electrons from the ligand (in this case, the N-CQDs) to the metal center (Fe), leading to enhanced absorption in the visible spectrum, thereby further fine-tuned the performance of the composite catalyst [41].

As a typical direct bandgap semiconductor (n = 1), the bandgap of CN、NCCN、FNCCN can be estimated according to the Tauc equation (Eq. (S1)). In Fig. 6b, FNCCN synthesized by Fe, N-CQDs and urea had the narrowest energy gap of 2.18 eV, which was lower than that of NCCN (2.26 eV) and CN (2.60 eV). This indicated that FNCCN possessed the higher photocatalytic activity, which can absorb and utilize more visible light, and thus generate more photogenerated carriers.

The VB of CN, NCCN and FNCCN were 2.16 eV, 1.87 eV and 1.82 eV respectively (Fig. 6c). The calculation of the CB position of the photocatalyst was performed using the formula (Eq. (S2)), and the schematic structure were shown in Fig. 6d. The schematic diagram assisted the analysis of the generation mechanism of free radicals in the following.

Compared with CN and NCCN, FNCCN exhibited a higher photocurrent density (Fig. 6e) and the smaller radius of the Nyquist plots (Fig. 6f). This result confirms that the tight connection formed between Fe, N-CQDs and g-C3N4 through the FeN bonds, which effectively promoted the charge transport rate of CN. The charge separation, transfer and recombination processes of photocatalysts were investigated through PL emission spectra and TRPL spectra [31]. Weaker PL intensity tended to imply lower carrier recombination rates [42]. The photoluminescence (PL) intensity of CN, NCCN, and FNCCN decreased in sequence (Fig. 6 g), suggesting that N-CQDs and Fe, N-CQDs could serve as electron acceptors and suppressed the recombination of electron-hole pairs within the catalyst [43]. Fe, N-CQDs can further reduce the rate of electron − hole pair complexation produced by the material upon illumination. To put it differently, the heteroatoms Fe and N, possessing electron-withdrawing characteristics, can transform CQDs into an electron buffer, thereby enhancing the efficiency of charge transport in CN. Interestingly, the peak positions of FNCCN and NCCN were redshifted by 7 nm relative to CN, which was caused by the excellent up-conversion photoluminescence performance of Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs. Table S2. showed the relevant parameters of the TRPL spectra of CN, NCCN and FNCCN. The average lifetime of carriers can be determined using the following equation (Eq. (S3)) [44]. From Fig. 6 h, the average emission decay time of FNCCN (4.546 ns) was close to that of NCCN (4.995 ns) and much lower than CN (6.159 ns). This elucidated that the incorporation of Fe, N-CQDs can diminish the likelihood of carrier recombination, allowing a greater number of photogenerated electrons to engage in the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, the solid EPR spectrum of the photocatalyst, as shown in Figure 6i, displayed identical g-value (g = 2.003) for all three materials, indicating the presence of unpaired electrons within the conjugated clusters of the carbon nitride aromatic rings [30,45]. The introduction of Fe, N-CQDs and N-CQDs can provide additional unsaturated sites, facilitating the formation of unpaired electrons in the material. Compared to CN and NCCN, FNCCN exhibited the strongest EPR signal, suggesting that FNCCN contained the most unpaired electrons, which was beneficial for photo-redox reactions.

SMX was selected as the pollutant to explore the degradation effect of different catalysts in different systems. As displayed in Fig. 7 (a–b), PMS + VIS was able to remove only 7.1 % (k = 0.0010 min−1) of SMX within 60 min without any catalyst addition. The removal of SMX was not substantial when depending solely on the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst. Among them, the degradation efficiency of FNCCN was 20.9 % (k = 0.0021 min−1), slightly surpassing that of NCCN (18.1 %, k = 0.0021 min−1) and CN (10.6 %, k = 0.0012 min−1). This were mainly due to the fact that Fe doping can effectively improve the up-conversion photoluminescence performance of N-CQDs. When further combined with urea, the photo-response range of FNCCN was expanded, and the photogenerated electron transport speed was accelerated. These improved optical properties ultimately resulted in improved photocatalytic degradation performance, aligning with the observations in 3.3.

Adding PMS as oxidant, the NCCN + PMS and FNCCN + PMS system can reach the removal rates of SMX attaining 59.06 % (k = 0.0126 min−1) and 83.96 % (k = 0.0266 min−1) within 60 min, respectively. The difference in degradation performance indicated that Fe doping in Fe, N-CQDs could markedly enhance the electron transfer ability of FNCCN. Moreover, the charge transfer efficiency of various systems was assessed the current–time curves in dark conditions (Fig. 7c). Compared to CN and NCCN, the addition of PMS resulted in the most significant change in current density for FNCCN at 100 s. This indicated that the redox cycle of Fe(II)/Fe(III) might be responsible for PMS activation in the dark，endowing it the fastest charge transfer rate from catalyst to PMS. It is worth noting that FNCCN also had the most drastic current density change after the addition of SMX at 400 s, suggesting that the system of FNCCN + PMS can generate the most ROS for charge exchange with SMX [46].

Notably, under visible light irrigation, the presence of CN, NCCN, or FNCCN, along with PMS, all contributed to an increased removal rate of SMX. In particular, FNCCN + PMS + vis achieved a removal rate of 99.54 % for SMX within 60 min, much higher than that of FNCCN + PMS (83.96 %, k = 0.0266 min−1) and FNCCN + vis (20.9 %, k = 0.0021 min−1). The synergy index of FNCCN was calculated according to Eq. (S4), and the result was 3.146 > 1, which indicated that there was a synergistic effect of photocatalytic and heterogeneous Fenton reactions in the system [47]. To explore the role of Fe in FNCCN + PMS + Vis system, the contribution factor (F) was used to quantify the role of Fe based on the synergistic index E (Eq. (S5)). The calculated contribution factor F was 1.612, which was higher than 1, indicating that Fe significantly bolstered the activation of PMS within the system, exerting a notably positive influence on the degradation of SMX [48].

In addition, we recovered the samples after 40 min of reaction in the FNCCN + dark, FNCCN + vis, and FNCCN + vis + PMS systems and performed XPS analysis. In accordance with the obtained Fe 2p XPS spectra, the ratio of Fe3+/Fe2+ were calculated as 49.56 %, 41.18 %, and 53.57 %, respectively (Fig. S6). In light of the preceding experimental outcomes, it was clearly that light conditions can accelerate the electron transfer in FNCCN, promote the valence conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+ in the system and ensure the continued charge transfer from g-C3N4 to Fe, N-CQDs via the FeN bonds in the presence of PMS. The Fe3+/Fe2+ redox cycle acted as a bridge for electrons consumption and PMS functioned as the final electron acceptors, which synergistically prompted the separation of charge carriers in g-C3N4.

Referring to the effect of Fe, N-CQDs doping amount, the degradation efficiency of FNCCN-5 (98.6 %) was found more obvious than that of FNCN-3 (87.4 %) in 40 min. This were due to two reasons. Firstly, the iron content of FNCCN increased with the rising concentration of Fe, N-CQDs, which led to an augmentation in the number of active sites participating in the reaction, resulting in the generation of more ROS. Secondly, the optimal quantity of Fe, N-CQDs can enhance both the optical characteristics and photocatalytic performance of FNCCN. Compared to FNCCN-5, the increase in SMX degradation efficiency of FNCCN-9 was not as pronounced. This was due to the fact that even though an augmentation in the quantity of Fe, N-CQDs allowed more iron species to participate in PMS activation, excessive doping also could torture the structure of CN, which was not only detrimental to photocarriers generation and electron transfer, but also led to an inner filter effect and significantly reduced the photocatalytic efficiency of FNCCN [33]. Considering the factors of degradation efficiency and resource utilization, FNCCN-5 was used in this experiment for further experiments below.

The effect of experimental parameters was carried out to determine the optimal conditions for SMX degradation in FNCCN + PMS + vis system. In General, under the condition of fixed parameters, the degradation efficiency of SMX in the system can be improved by increasing the amount of catalyst and persulfate. The removal rate of SMX rose as the quantity of FNCCN increased, and the rate constant escalated from 0.0363 min−1 to 0.0963 min−1, signifying that the active site of the catalyst was the limiting factor in the degradation reaction (Fig. 7d and Fig. S7a). As the catalyst dosage increased from 0.4 g/L to 0.5 g/L, the enhancement in the degradation rate diminished, which may be due to the fact that excessive FNCCN particles shielding the light transmission, thus reduced the photodegradation efficiency of FNCCN and the valence conversion of Fe. As the PMS concentration rose from 0 g/L to 0.5 g/L, the degradation of SMX was expedited, resulting in a rise in the removal rate from 17.18 % to 98.64 % in 40 min (Fig. 7e and Fig. S7b). In Fig. 7f and Fig. S7c, when the concentration of SMX was lower than 5 ppm, SMX was completely degraded within 40 min. Nevertheless, when the concentration was raised to 10 ppm, the removal rate decreased to 92.11 %. The pH value of the solution had negligible effect on the degradation of SMX (Fig. 7 g and Fig. S7d) in this experiment. However, the pH of the solution affected the dissociation form of the contaminant and the surface state of the catalyst. FNCCN was positive charged at pH < 6.48 and negative charged at pH > 6.48 with (Fig. 7 h and Fig. S7e), while SMX generally dissociated and became negatively charged at pH > 5.4 [49]. Therefore, when the pH value was adjusted between 5.4 and 6.48, there was electrostatic attraction between SMX and FNCCN, which may account for the better adsorption and degradation of FNCCN at pH = 6 than other pH values.

In practical applications, some inorganic anions and natural organic compounds were coexisted in the water matrix, which may interfere with the oxidation of organic pollutants through different mechanisms. The existence of Cl- led to a slight enhancement in the degradation rate of SMX when compared to the control experiment, as depicted in Fig. 7i. This owed to the reactions between PMS and Cl- to generate HOCl and Cl2, which promoted the degradation of SMX (Eq. (2)–(3)) [26]. All inorganic anions except Cl- showed inhibition effect toward SMX degradation, especially for HCO3 –, which reduced the degradation rate of SMX down to 88.46 % in 40 min. The reason was that HCO3 – acted as a scavenger of reactive species in advanced oxidation processes, consuming
OH or SO4
− and transforming into CO3
− (Eq. (4)–(5)) [50]. In addition, the presence of HA inhibited the removal of SMX, and the degradation rate of SMX decreased to 78.93 % in 40 min. On one hand, HA with oxygenated functional groups can directly consume PMS and photogenerated electrons. On the other hand, HA could compete with SMX for reactive oxygen species in the aqueous phase, resulting in a reduction in the quantity of ROS involved in the degradation reactions and the subsequent degradation efficiency of SMX [51].
(2) HSO5 − + Cl− → SO4 2− + HOCl
(3) HOCl + H+ + Cl− → Cl2 + H2O
(4) HCO3 − +
OH → CO3
− + H2O
(5) HCO3 − + SO4
− → CO3
− + SO4 2- + H+

Metal leaching from solid catalysts often results in secondary environmental pollution and significant deactivation of the catalyst. We performed several cyclic degradation experiments using FNCCN and subsequently utilized Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry (ICP-MS) to accurately determine the concentration of Fe ions in the solution after each reaction. The results were illustrated in Fig. S8a and S8b, respectively. Following five consecutive cycles, the concentration of leached iron progressively decreased, with a maximum value of 242.3 µg/L. This was significantly lower than the national standard limit value (10.0 mg/L, GB/T 31962-2015), indicating that the pollution caused by FNCCN was controllable. Concurrently, the degradation of SMX over 40 min only decreased from 98.64 % to 83.21 % after 5 cycles reuse. These results indicated that FNCCN possessed a good reusability. As the cycle count increased, the removal rate of FNCCN for SMX gradually decreased. Despite a decrease in the removal rate of SMX by approximately 15 % in the fifth cycle compared to the initial cycle, FNCCN demonstrated remarkable reusability by maintaining a relatively high removal efficiency of 85.3 %. This observation underscored the stability and durability of FNCCN, suggesting its potential for sustainable applications in the treatment of various contaminants. Furthermore, we performed XRD analyses on FNCCN both prior to and subsequent to the reaction, followed by a comparative evaluation. As depicted in Fig. S8c, the post-reaction XRD spectrum of FNCCN closely mirrors its pre-reaction counterpart, exhibiting negligible alterations in characteristic peaks. This indicated that the crystalline structure of FNCCN can be well-maintained during the reaction process, demonstrating its excellent stability.

EPR spectroscopy was used to identify ROS in different systems. It was well known that DMPO in aqueous solution act as capture agent for
OH and SO4
−. But in fact, the signal peaks of DMPO-
OH and DMPO-SO4
− were not observed in the FNCCN + PMS + vis and FNCCN + PMS + dark systems. Instead, there appeared seven peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:2:1:2:1:2:1 in Fig. 8 a, this was considered to be the signal peak of DMPO-X [52]. The presence of DMPO-X can be ascribed to the generation of potent oxidizing agents during the activation of PMS by FNCCN, which can directly oxidize DMPO. Compared to the conditions in dark, FNCCN + PMS + vis exhibited a higher peak intensity of DMPO-X signals, indicating that light conditions can promote the generation of stronger oxidized substances by FNCCN + PMS. Moreover, it should be noted that the DMPO-X signals can not exclude the existence of either
OH or SO4
− production in FNCCN + PMS + vis systems. They may also contributed to the SMX degradation, but it would not be the major ROS [53]. DMPO in methanol solution can be used as a spin-trapping agent for
O2 –. The characteristic peak of
O2 – was clearly observed in the EPR spectrum under light conditions, demonstrating that the catalyst can produce
O2 – to degrade SMX under light conditions (Fig 8b).

The intensity of characteristic peak signals of 1O2 gradually decreased in the sequence of vis + FNCCN + PMS, dark + FNCCN + PMS, vis + FNCCN and vis + PMS (Fig. 8c). There was no obvious TEMP-1O2 signals in vis + PMS. This suggested that PMS as the precursor for generating ROS, played a significant role in the generation of 1O2.

The quenching experiments were conducted in this study to further elucidate the mechanism in the FNCCN + PMS + vis system. In Fig. 8 (d–e), upon the introduction of L-histidine and EDTA-2Na, the degradation rate of SMX decreased rapidly after 40 min, from 98.64 % (k = 0.0963 min−1) to 36.61 % (k = 0.0095 min−1) and 31.10 % (k = 0.0079 min−1), respectively, suggesting that 1O2 and h+ were the major active substance. Moreover, the degradation rate of SMX was less pronounced in Vis + FNCCN system (Fig. 7a), indicating that the generated h+ and
O2 – were inefficient to degrade SMX directly without PMS. The possible reason for the sharp decline in the degradation rate of SMX after adding EDTA-2Na was that h+ participated in the generation of 1O2 [54].

Following the addition of p-BQ and Kr2Cr2O7, the degradation efficiencies of SMX decreased to 66.9 % (k = 0.0227 min−1) and 77.9 % (k = 0.0322 min−1), respectively. These results indicated that
O2 – and e- exerted subsidiary effects toward the photocatalytic degradation of SMX. The SMX degradation after adding p-BQ and N2 + p-BQ were compared, and it was found there was no significant difference. It might suggest that
O2 – were not the entire resultant of reduced O2 generated by trapping e-.

Notably, the degradation of SMX was slightly inhibited by the addition of MeOH, which was in consistent with EPR results that
OH and SO4
− were not the main active species during the reactions. However, when phenol replaced MeOH, the degradation rate of SMX was significantly decreased. The contrary results probably ascribed to the fact that the radicals were produced and functioned mainly on that catalyst surface and MeOH failed to quench the attached free radicals [55]. The introduction of IPA still maintained a high removal rate of 88.66 % (k = 0.0495 min−1), excluding the role of
OH as dominant ROS. In Fig. 6c, the VB of FNCCN was −1.82 eV, which was lower than –OH/
OH (-1.99 eV). Therefore,
OH can not be generated by the oxidation of H2O by h+ [10]. Drawing conclusions from the capture experiments and EPR results presented above, it can be deduced that 1O2 and h+ were the primary ROS accountable for the degradation of SMX in the FNCCN + PMS + vis system.

These findings led to the development of a potential mechanism for contaminant degradation within the FNCCN + PMS + visible light system, which was depicted in Fig. 9 . The doping of Fe atom in N-CQDs allowed the existence of both Fe(II) and Fe(III) through FeN ligand. The close connection between Fe, N-CQDs and g-C3N4 was established owing to the Fe atoms coordination with N in g-C3N4. Lone pair of electrons of N in g-C3N4 can easily chelate with unoccupied orbitals of Fe with variable valence, thus forming an high-speed electron transfer bridge between CN and Fe, N-CQDs, which were able to participate in the activation of PMS even under dark conditions (Eq. (6)–(8)) [56]. Fe, N-CQDs had up-conversion photo-luminescence effects, excellent electron transfer and reservoir properties, which extended the light absorption range of the composite photocatalysts and inhibited the complexation of photogenerated carriers. FNCCN generated electrons and holes under the irradiation of light (Eq. (9)). Photogenerated electrons were immediately transferred by conjunct Fe, N-CQDs and withdrawn by Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle for PMS activation (Eq. (10)–(11)) [54]. In this way, the separation for photogenerated carriers could be accelerated and the “electron transfer bridge” formed. In addition,
OH and
O2 – can be generated through the Eq. (12) to (16). As the predominant ROS within the system, 1O2 can be generated through the reaction and conversion of O2/
O2 – and h+ or
O2 – and SO4
− (Eq. (17)–(18)) [47,54]. The SMX was oxidized and degraded to CO2 and H2O by the action of aforementioned ROS such as 1O2, SO4
−,
O2 –, and
OH (Eq. (19)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation intermediate and toxicity analysis of SMX
ce:section.ce:para: In the FNCCN + Vis + PMS system, the removal rate of TOC within 40 min was only 40.58 %, indicating that a large number of intermediate products were generated during the degradation process of SMX (Fig. S9a). The decomposition products of SMX were analyzed by LC-MS, and combined with the calculation results of Fukui-index (Table S3), four possible degradation pathways of SMX in the system were proposed. The optimized molecular structure of SMX was shown in Fig. S9b. In pathway I, the S-N bond of SMX was vulnerable to nucleophilic attack, which caused S8-N7 to break to generate P1 with m/z values of 99. Conjugation and hydroxylation can occur simultaneously on the P1 [55]. On the one hand, the –NH2 group produced P2 through the coupling reaction, while the reactive oxygen species were able to further oxidize P2 (m/z = 193) to form P3 (m/z = 209). On the other hand, the surface hydroxylation of P1 generated P4 (m/z = 115) and P5 (m/z = 133). In pathway II, N15 was the most active site in SMX, with an f- value of 0.127. It was easily attacked by electrophilic reactive oxygen species such as h+ and 1O2 to form P6 (m/z = 284), and further oxidized and degraded to P7 (m/z = 99). In pathway III, SMX formed hydroxylation product P8 (m/z = 270) at C6, which broke the S8-N7 bond to generate P9 (m/z = 156) under the continuous attack of reactive oxygen species. In pathway IV, SMX was substituted by hydroxyl group to form P10 (m/z = 284) during the electrophilic reaction, and the hydroxyl group was broken to generate P11(m/z = 239) [57]. Ultimately, some intermediates were oxidatively degraded into small organic molecules and further mineralized into CO2 and H2O [46] (Fig. 10 ).

Based on the toxicity analysis of SMX and its degradation products in Fig. S10, it was observed that the toxicity of most intermediates decreased during SMX degradation, but some intermediates still increased. Therefore, the degradation time in practical applications needed to be extended to achieve a higher level of mineralization and oxidation, ensuring that the toxicity of pollutants was reduced during degradation.

In this study, a novel hybrid photocatalyst (FNCCN) by incorporating Fe, N-CQDs into g-C3N4 via FeN bridge was fabricated by hydrothermal and thermal polymerization methods. Under light irradiation, the FNCCN demonstrated excellent PMS activation and SMX degradation properties. The removal rate of SMX reached 98.61 % in 40 min, which was approximately 27.6 % and 40.4 % higher than that of NCCN and CN. The enhanced performance was attributed to the covalent modification of g-C3N4 by Fe, N-CQDs, which helped to facilitate the formation of FeN covalent bond and thus the boosted light absorption capacity and close connectivity. The Fe3+/Fe2+ cycling via PMS activation is designed as an engine for faster electron transfer which in turn ensured the continuous activation of PMS. Radical trapping experiments and EPR results confirmed the dominant roles of 1O2 and h+ in the degradation process. In addition, the stability of the composite was verified through cyclic experiments and the possible degradation pathways as well as toxicity of intermediate products for SMX was analyzed. This work may provide new insights for designing more efficient CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalysts for light induced PMS activation and water purification.
